agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request to integrate Warehouse Management (Context A) and Sales/Order Management (Context B) by blocking orders when stock is insufficient and showing real-time stock availability. This is a cross-bounded-context integration that fundamentally changes the autonomy of Context B, which currently accepts orders without checking stock.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management. The request threatens to collapse two distinct bounded contexts into a coupled monolith if not handled carefully.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"StockLevel","definition":"An integer >= 0 in Context A representing the current quantity of a Product on hand in the warehouse. Owned exclusively by Warehouse Management."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A sales entity in Context B representing a customer's intent to purchase one or more products. Currently accepted unconditionally."},{"term":"OrderLine","definition":"A line item within an Order referencing a Product ID and a desired Quantity. Lives in Context B."},{"term":"Stock Reservation","definition":"A domain concept MISSING from both contexts — a temporary hold on stock allocated to a pending order, preventing double-selling. Must be introduced to correctly implement this feature."},{"term":"Stock Available Indicator","definition":"A read-model projection of Context A's StockLevel into Context B for display purposes. Must be eventually consistent, NOT treated as authoritative for blocking decisions."},{"term":"Order Blocking","definition":"The act of rejecting or holding an Order when the requested Quantity exceeds available (unreserved) StockLevel. This is a NEW cross-context business rule."},{"term":"Available Stock","definition":"StockLevel minus all active Stock Reservations. This is the correct quantity to check against — NOT raw StockLevel."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"StockLevel in Warehouse Management must never go below zero.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Negative stock represents a physically impossible state. If violated, the system's data integrity is corrupt — you cannot ship items that do not exist.","testable_assertion":"For any Product, StockLevel >= 0 at all times, including under concurrent operations."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Context B must NOT directly write to or hold a transactional lock on Context A's StockLevel. The two bounded contexts must remain autonomous and communicate via integration events or an anti-corruption layer.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Violating bounded context autonomy creates tight coupling that corrupts both domain models. If Sales can directly mutate StockLevel, the Warehouse model loses its integrity guarantees.","testable_assertion":"No code path in Context B directly modifies StockLevel in Context A. All cross-context communication goes through a defined integration mechanism (events, API calls, or an ACL)."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Order blocking must be based on Available Stock (StockLevel minus active Reservations), not raw StockLevel.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Using raw StockLevel without reservations allows double-selling under concurrency. Two simultaneous orders for the last item would both pass the check. This is a data consistency invariant.","testable_assertion":"When two orders for Quantity=1 arrive concurrently for a Product with StockLevel=1, exactly one is blocked and one succeeds. The system never commits two reservations that exceed StockLevel."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The 'stock available' indicator shown to the sales team is eventually consistent and must NOT be used as the authoritative source for order blocking decisions.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A read-model projection can be stale. If blocking decisions rely on the UI indicator rather than a transactional check at order placement time, race conditions will cause overselling or false rejections. The indicator and the blocking check are two different mechanisms.","testable_assertion":"The stock indicator can show 'available' while an order placed at the same moment is correctly blocked due to a concurrent reservation. The two are decoupled."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"Stock Reservations must have a time-to-live (TTL) or explicit release mechanism to prevent indefinite stock lockup from abandoned or failed orders.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The TTL duration is a business policy decision (e.g., 15 minutes, 30 minutes). The need for expiry is critical, but the specific timeout is adjustable by policy.","testable_assertion":"A Stock Reservation that is neither confirmed nor explicitly released is automatically released after the configured TTL, and the stock becomes available again."},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"A blocked order must communicate a clear reason to the customer and the sales team, including which OrderLine(s) had insufficient stock and the current available quantity.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a UX and communication policy — the business could decide to show more or less detail. But some form of feedback is required for the feature to be useful.","testable_assertion":"When an order is blocked, the response includes at minimum: the Product ID(s) that failed, the requested Quantity, and the available Quantity at the time of the check."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A Product with Available Stock of 10 units","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for 5 units of that Product","then":"The Order is accepted, and a Stock Reservation of 5 units is created, reducing Available Stock to 5."},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A Product with Available Stock of 3 units","when":"A customer places an Order with an OrderLine for 5 units of that Product","then":"The Order is blocked with a clear reason indicating insufficient stock (requested: 5, available: 3)."},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A Product with Available Stock of 1 unit","when":"Two customers simultaneously place Orders each requesting 1 unit","then":"Exactly one Order is accepted and one is blocked. StockLevel never goes below 0 and no double-selling occurs."},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A Stock Reservation exists for 5 units and the reservation TTL expires","when":"The TTL elapses without order confirmation","then":"The reservation is released and the 5 units are returned to Available Stock."},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A Product with StockLevel of 20 in Context A","when":"The sales team views the product in Context B","then":"A 'stock available' indicator is shown reflecting an eventually-consistent projection of Available Stock. The indicator refreshes within a defined SLA (e.g., seconds, not minutes)."},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Context A's StockLevel changes due to a supplier receipt","when":"The change is persisted in Context A","then":"An integration event is emitted and Context B's stock indicator is updated within the eventual consistency SLA. No direct database query from B to A occurs."},{"id":"AC-7","given":"An Order with multiple OrderLines","when":"One OrderLine has sufficient stock but another does not","then":"The entire Order is blocked (atomic check across all lines). Partial acceptance is not permitted unless explicitly enabled by policy."}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Product exists in Context B but has never been stocked in Context A (no StockLevel record).","expected_behavior":"Treat as zero stock. Order is blocked. The stock indicator shows 'unavailable'. The system does not error — absence of stock data means no stock."},{"scenario":"StockLevel is updated (supplier receipt) between the stock check and the reservation commit.","expected_behavior":"The reservation mechanism must be transactional within Context A. The check-and-reserve must be atomic. An optimistic concurrency or pessimistic lock approach ensures correctness."},{"scenario":"A customer places an order, stock is reserved, then the customer cancels.","expected_behavior":"The reservation is explicitly released, and Available Stock is restored. Cancellation must trigger reservation cleanup."},{"scenario":"Context A is temporarily unavailable (network partition, downtime).","expected_behavior":"Context B must decide on a degradation policy: either reject all orders (safe but disruptive) or accept orders optimistically and reconcile later (risky but available). This is a critical business decision that must be made explicitly."},{"scenario":"A product is discontinued in Context A but still listed in Context B.","expected_behavior":"The stock indicator should show zero/unavailable. Orders for discontinued products should be blocked with a specific reason, not just 'insufficient stock'."},{"scenario":"Bulk order: a single OrderLine requests more units than have ever existed in the warehouse.","expected_behavior":"Blocked like any insufficient-stock scenario. No special handling needed, but the error message should not be misleading."},{"scenario":"Reservation TTL expires and stock is released, but the customer then tries to confirm/pay.","expected_behavior":"The confirmation attempt must re-check and re-reserve. If stock is no longer available, the customer is informed that their cart has expired and stock is no longer guaranteed."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Blocking oversold orders eliminates costly backorder fulfillment, refund processing, and customer compensation. However, false rejections due to stale data or overly aggressive locking could cause lost sales. The reservation TTL policy directly impacts conversion rates — too short loses customers, too long locks inventory.","operational":"Introduces a new Stock Reservation concept that must be managed (creation, expiry, cleanup). Adds operational coupling between two previously independent contexts — Context A downtime now directly impacts Context B's ability to accept orders. Requires monitoring of reservation expiry, event lag, and cross-context health.","user":"Sales team gains real-time stock visibility, reducing manual calls to warehouse. Customers get immediate feedback instead of post-purchase cancellation emails. However, eventually-consistent indicators may show stale data, confusing the sales team if they see 'available' but the order is blocked.","risk":"HIGH — This integration introduces tight temporal coupling between contexts. The biggest risk is the degradation policy when Context A is unavailable. Without an explicit decision, the system may silently fail in production. Concurrency bugs in the reservation mechanism can cause either overselling (revenue loss + customer trust) or phantom blocking (lost sales). Additionally, the real-time indicator creates a false sense of certainty for an eventually-consistent value.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Orders may be rejected at placement time instead of accepted and later cancelled. Better experience overall, but frustrating if the indicator showed 'available' moments before."},{"group":"Sales Team","impact":"Gains a stock indicator improving confidence and reducing manual checks. Must be trained that the indicator is approximate, not guaranteed."},{"group":"Warehouse Team","impact":"Must now manage Stock Reservations as a new concept. Reservation expiry and cleanup add operational overhead."},{"group":"Engineering / Platform","impact":"Must implement cross-context integration without violating bounded context autonomy. Needs event infrastructure, reservation storage, TTL management, and a degradation strategy."},{"group":"Finance / Revenue","impact":"Reduced refund and backorder costs. Potential lost sales from false rejections must be monitored as a KPI."}]},"confidence":{"level":52,"reasoning":"The spec is a one-sentence feature request with significant ambiguity. It does not specify: (1) the degradation policy when Context A is unavailable, (2) whether partial order acceptance is allowed, (3) the consistency SLA for the stock indicator, (4) whether 'block' means reject outright or hold for review, (5) the reservation TTL policy. These are all critical business decisions that cannot be inferred from the request alone. The cross-context integration pattern is also unspecified — events vs. synchronous API vs. shared database — and each has profoundly different domain implications.","high_confidence_areas":["StockLevel >= 0 invariant","Need for Stock Reservation concept to prevent double-selling","Bounded context autonomy must be preserved","Eventually-consistent indicator must not be used for blocking decisions","Concurrency scenario with simultaneous orders"],"low_confidence_areas":["Degradation policy when Context A is unavailable","Whether 'block' means reject or hold-for-review","Partial order acceptance policy (some lines pass, some fail)","Reservation TTL duration","Consistency SLA for the stock indicator","Whether the feature applies to all products or only certain categories"],"assumptions":["'Block' means reject the order at placement time with a clear reason, not queue it for manual review","The entire order is blocked if any line has insufficient stock (atomic)","Stock Reservations are a new concept that does not yet exist in either context","The integration should use domain events, not direct database access","The stock indicator is for the sales team's internal tooling, not customer-facing"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The feature request says 'automatically block it' but does not define what 'block' means. Options: (A) Reject the order outright and inform the customer immediately, (B) Accept the order into a 'pending-stock-review' state for manual sales team intervention, (C) Hold the order silently and auto-release when stock arrives. Each has very different domain modeling and UX implications.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["A: Reject outright at placement time","B: Accept into held/review state","C: Silent hold with auto-fulfillment on restock"],"recommendation":"Option A — reject outright. It is the simplest, most honest to the customer, and avoids creating a 'limbo' order state that complicates the Order lifecycle. But this MUST be confirmed by the business."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"No degradation policy specified for when Context A (Warehouse) is unavailable. This is a critical architectural and business decision: should Context B stop accepting ALL orders, or accept them optimistically and risk overselling?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Finance","Engineering"],"options":["Fail-closed: reject all orders when stock service is unavailable","Fail-open: accept orders optimistically, reconcile later","Circuit-breaker with fallback: use cached stock levels for a grace period, then fail-closed"],"recommendation":"Circuit-breaker with cached fallback. Pure fail-closed kills revenue during outages. Pure fail-open defeats the purpose of the feature. A time-bounded fallback to last-known stock levels balances both. Requires business sign-off on the cache staleness tolerance."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"Partial order acceptance is unspecified. If an order has 3 lines and only 1 has insufficient stock, should the entire order be blocked or should the 2 sufficient lines be accepted?","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Sales Team","Warehouse Team"],"options":["Atomic: block entire order if any line fails","Partial: accept available lines, block insufficient ones","Split: create two orders — one fulfillable, one blocked"],"recommendation":"Atomic blocking (entire order). Partial acceptance creates split-shipment complexity and confusing customer communication. But this is a business call."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The 'real-time' requirement for the stock indicator lacks a defined SLA. What latency is acceptable? Sub-second (requires WebSocket/SSE push), a few seconds (polling), or near-real-time within 30 seconds (event-driven projection)?","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Team","Engineering"],"options":["Sub-second via WebSocket push from Context A events","1-5 second polling of a read-model projection","30-second eventual consistency via async event processing"],"recommendation":"Event-driven projection with 1-5 second latency. True real-time is expensive and unnecessary — the sales team needs directional accuracy, not millisecond precision. The indicator should show a timestamp of last update to set expectations."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Any implementation that has Context B directly querying or writing to Context A's database violates bounded context autonomy.","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"Any implementation that checks raw StockLevel without a reservation mechanism will allow double-selling under concurrency.","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"Any implementation that uses the stock indicator's cached value as the authoritative source for blocking decisions will produce incorrect results under load.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"Any implementation that creates Stock Reservations without a TTL or release mechanism will cause phantom stock lockup and revenue loss.","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"}]}

This is a cross-bounded-context integration that fundamentally changes the autonomy of Context B, which currently accepts orders without checking stock.","bounded_context":"Cross-context integration between Warehouse Management and Sales/Order Management.
